speaker: interviewer
text: Tape six, December 18, 1996. Interview with Aron Bell. You came to Paris.

speaker: interviewee
text: Came to Paris. And, again, I found a group [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] of kids like me-- other words, teenagers. And I was there for a few days. And somebody there got me a job in a kitchen. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Then I was a king because I earned money.

speaker: interviewer
text: Inside the-- the--

speaker: interviewee
text: In Paris.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the city?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the city, of course. I got a place in a Jewish restaurant to work in the kitchen. So the first thing I did, I ate, you know, hunt and shot a whole chicken. And the boss came in while I was eating it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I got scared because I'm eating the chicken that he's going to fire me. So I stopped eating. He says, don't worry. Don't be afraid. I'm not going to fire you for that. Eat as much as your heart desires.

speaker: interviewee
text: There were very nice Jews in every-- maybe I was lucky. I don't know. I got across very nice people in France, in England.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was this man? Did-- did he survive the war in Europe, too?

speaker: interviewee
text: Which man?

speaker: interviewer
text: Your boss.

speaker: interviewee
text: I wouldn't know about that. I don't know. I would assume he survived the war someplace in, in Western Europe, not in Eastern.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know French?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. But it took two, three months, and I spoke French. In those days, a young kid is very easy to, to learn.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you stay in Paris?

speaker: interviewee
text: Till they are arrested me and threw me the hell out. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Who arrested you?

speaker: interviewee
text: One day, they checked the passport. I had no passport. I had no documents. I had some sort of a piece of paper just to go into France, just to cross the border. Then I was told I should throw it away. So that's what I--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who gave you that piece of paper?

speaker: interviewee
text: [POINTS] At that group where I was in Italy. And I did that, and I was no, no documents whatsoever.

speaker: interviewer
text: You told them in that place that you lived in Italy that you wanted to go to France?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they issued you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Gave me a piece of paper, who I am-- piece of paper. In those days, they were very mild, the borders. It's not like today, particularly in Europe. I mean, that's my personal opinion because otherwise--

speaker: interviewer
text: So they put you in jail in Paris.

speaker: interviewee
text: Behind bars in a police station.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what--

speaker: interviewee
text: And I got very scared. I was afraid they shouldn't send me back to Russia. So they were talking to me. I don't understand. I said, like this, nothing. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I think it was the following day, not longer than that, they brought in a Jewish man-- tall, handsome as they come. He started to speak to me Yiddish. When I-- started speak to me Yiddish, I looked at him like my [YIDDISH], like Messiah. [LAUGHS] And he took me out. He took me to his house. I stayed there for a while, found me work.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of work?

speaker: interviewee
text: [DRINKS] Delivering bread from a-- from the truck into the store, like a delivery.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was that man?

speaker: interviewee
text: Was a rabbi's son from Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. I don't remember. He's not alive by now. Don't forget, I'm going back--

speaker: interviewer
text: Had you known him before?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Where do I know-- no.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did he know you were in jail?

speaker: interviewee
text: Cops brought him-- the police. At those days, in those countries like Germany or France or Holland, that was a daily matter in those days that teenagers like me are roaming around without a home. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They don't know where to go. They don't know what to do-- whatever. Were thousands. I wasn't the only one. But then I was already OK. When I-- took me out of jail, and he gave me-- he made some sort of a document. And I think they let me stay. And they gave me a visa-- permission-- to stay in France, I don't know, a few months. Then when expired, I had to leave.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where am I gonna go? So I wanted to stay there. So when my document expired, I was sitting in a restaurant, and they closed the doors front and back. You can't run out. Whoever was sitting there, they checked the documents. I have nothing. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] So they arrested me again, but this was easy. Then I wasn't afraid anymore because I knew I'm not a criminal. I already got smart with the police.

speaker: interviewee
text: I wasn't afraid. I knew that they wouldn't touch me. It's only a matter to get a passport or whatever you want to call it-- some sort to legitimize myself. This was-- this was OK already. I lived like this with my friends in England till 1948.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you go to England?

speaker: interviewee
text: When France threw me the hell out. [LAUGHS] When my visa was up, I went to London. London threw me out. I went back to Paris. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I was wandering like that between London and Paris till 1948.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do in London?

speaker: interviewee
text: London, I worked [DRINKS] in a factory until I got caught again. My visa expired.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you live?

speaker: interviewee
text: With a friend that we pushed through the Second World War together in the woods.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: He-- he got ahead of me. His name is Kagan. He's writing a book now.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's his first name? Julius [NON-ENGLISH]. We pushed through the Second World War together in [BACKGROUND NOISE] Eastern Europe. So I stood with him for a while.

speaker: interviewee
text: My visa expired. I went back to Paris, then back again, doing-- I was roaming around like this between those two cities. I went to Switzerland for a while, for a couple of weeks. Then in '48, I went to Israel, Israeli army. I was 2 1/2 years in the army, till October 1950. And that's when I went back with an Israeli passport to France.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel about being in the Israeli army?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was great, except in the summer. It was very hot. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I assume-- I don't know exactly what you mean by that question, to feel in the Israeli army. It was a war, obvious. [SIGHS] All the Arab countries attacked Israel. And the Israelis had no arms compared to the Arabs. [BACKGROUND NOISE] But the Israelis were very capable, particularly the Israelis from the kibbutz. They, they fought the Arabs like the Russian soldiers fought the Nazis. Of course, it wasn't this type of a war like I went through. And therefore, this war wasn't already for me that scary, like for others. I did not accept this war as a war that I went through.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what way was that different?

speaker: interviewee
text: In what way? We were surrounded, about 80 people with arms. I'm talking about Israeli army now, beginning of the war in a-- in a village or a town called Harduf near Jerusalem. For some reason or another, Ben-Gurion did not want to give up that village. We were 80 men, 80 young men. For three months, we were surrounded there. We couldn't-- no delivery, no nothing. No food. We just lived on sardines and bread we had. Nothing else. In the morning, 4 o'clock in the morning, we were attacked every morning. [ARABIC], kill the Jews. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: That's what it means in Arabic?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. They used to holler it, [ARABIC], kill the Jews. But to me, this war really was not as frightening as in Eastern Europe that I went through, the Holocaust.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why was that less frightening?

speaker: interviewee
text: Why was not that frightening? [LAUGHS] I assume because I had experienced what-- about shooting, about a war, about fighting. This was a-- for some reason or another, I was not-- I didn't accept it as it-- as the Israelis did. At times, they looked at me as abnormal. They were worrying. I was laughing. That's the truth.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your job in the army?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in communications-- a radio. Other words, a two-way radio I had always, with a captain. I had it very good in the army. They-- I had it-- I was lucky maybe. I don't know. I had it very good in the Israeli army.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the Israeli government aware of your family background?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. They were not aware, and I would not revealed it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your brothers? Were they aware of--

speaker: interviewee
text: I think later on they were aware, yes, of my brothers, yes. But I never wanted to talk about the past.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how long--

speaker: interviewee
text: To me, the past was dead, up until four or five years back.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how long did you stay there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Till October 1950.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then?

speaker: interviewee
text: They gave me pockets full of money because all these 2 1/2 years, I didn't get paid. I didn't take the money that they had. And then they paid me in the one shot. When out of the army, they offered me to go to school. They wanted to give me an apartment. They wanted to give me a job with the electric company. That's-- over there, you get a job, that's for life with the electric company. I couldn't take the heat. And I left Israel. They gave me-- they paid me for over 2 1/2 years, whatever were dollars. I went back to France. But I had a passport. I was a king. I had documents. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is my story.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: I hope it's the end.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's not the end.

speaker: interviewee
text: The end for our interview.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then-- and then where did you come?

speaker: interviewee
text: From Paris? [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I met again that man that took me out of jail in 1945. Went to his house. Went to Switzerland. Couple of times, I brought watches from Switzerland, and I sold it. I made some money. Then he send me with his wife to Canada, divorced his wife because she was Polish actress. She saved him through the war. They got divorced. I went to Montreal.

speaker: interviewer
text: He sent you with his wife to Canada?

speaker: interviewee
text: With his wife, yeah, together on the same ship-- the same boat.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you mean, he sent you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Exactly what I said. He said, you want to go to Canada? Ana is going. He said-- he said, what are you worried? I'm paying the tickets. Free ride to Canada. I said, fine. And I'll get you, he says, a visa. I said, great. I'll go to Canada. [LAUGHS] And this is the way I got to Canada.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you get to Canada?

speaker: interviewee
text: Let's see. I would say sometimes like '51. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I was in Canada. I walk on the street. Fellow taps me on my shoulders-- one of the fellows that we lived through the war together. Took me to his house again. [? Bedzer ?] family. They live in Florida.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you come to the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: The first time? When I was in Canada, I bought myself a used car, and I went to America because I knew I have brothers here. One sister I had here, I think I knew already. So I came here for a visit New Year's, [LAUGHS] until I spend all my money. I was ashamed to tell my family that I'm broke.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I had a tank of gas. And wherever this tank of gas took me, back to Montreal. I went to Montreal. And there I got stuck just beyond the border. I just crossed the border into Montreal. Gas is out.

speaker: interviewee
text: Stopped near a gas station. I gave them the watch. I said, should give me a couple of gallons of gas to get to Montreal. And tomorrow I'll bring him the money. And he will return me the watch.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he did. I come home, and detectives surrounded me right away my car. Where is the jewelry that you're smuggling from America to Canada? [LAUGHS] I had no jewelry. I wasn't afraid of them. I knew that fellow in the gas station apparently told them that. When did you come here to live?

speaker: interviewee
text: For good? I think in '52, it was, I came here for good. And that's my story.

speaker: interviewer
text: What has been your job in the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I would rather stop at that. I would stop it. That's enough.

speaker: interviewer
text: How often do you think back to the times and they--

speaker: interviewee
text: Right now, [INTERPOSING VOICES] amazing, which amazing. I wish I would get across a person he should explain that to me, psychiatrist or a psychologist. Amazingly, that up until [SIGHS] five, 10 years back, I wanted to forget the past. I would cut it out of my mind totally. But now I live with the past. Now it's constantly on my mind for some reason or another. I, I don't know why.

speaker: interviewer
text: Constantly?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just about, yeah. Maybe on account of my age. I don't know. Constantly on my mind. Maybe on account of this because in the news media, you have now a lot about the survivors. Maybe that's what-- but now it's-- I-- I live with that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you wish to talk about your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think I told you enough.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any message for future generations?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm not that smart. Hopefully, Israel survives. I hope Netanyahu will lose his-- [LAUGHS] to be prime minister. Look at what goes on in Israel? I mean, you're asking me such a questions. How can answer? Look what goes on now.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have a personal message for future generations?

speaker: interviewee
text: Should be good Jews and good people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- would you like me to identify this picture? Please.

speaker: interviewee
text: From the right to the left, the first one is my brother, Asael, then a cousin of mine-- a girl-- then my brother, Zisl. Then it's me. Then it's Estelle, my sister, and Tuvia. And this picture is this is our mill and the bridge. And this is our land, what you see there.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken about in 1939 to '40.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where'd you get the pictures?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother had it. This is my brother-- I'm sorry-- Asael during the partisans years.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get those pictures?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken during the war by his wife. And I made it bigger from a small photograph.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Welcome.
